retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glucocorticoids -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The majority of the effects of glucocorticoids result from the binding of cortisol to intracellular glucocorticoid receptors (GR). This complex translocates in the nucleus to change gene expression in a variety of ways. Intracellular GR receptors are bound to stabilizing proteins like Hsp-90 in their steroid-free state.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteogenesis Imperfecta -- History and Physical -- Common Features
retrieved_docs.content: A defect in collagen chaperones that accompany procollagen molecules from ER to Golgi apparatus 2.Autosomal recessive [28] [29] Specific defects: SERPINH1 - type X, FKBP10 - type XI.

retrieved_docs.title: Intracoronary Stents -- Equipment -- Stent Types and Materials
retrieved_docs.content: DES consists of 3 components—a metallic stent platform, an active pharmacological drug agent, and a carrier vehicle. Stainless steel or cobalt-chromium is the most commonly used metal, providing long-term mechanical stability to counteract vascular recoil. Commonly used drugs work by blocking signal transduction and cell cycle progression at various phases, thereby inhibiting SMC proliferation or intimal hyperplasia at the stented arterial site. Rapamycin agents bind to the intracellular protein FKBP-12, which inhibits the protein kinase mTOR. Rapamycin agents bind to the intracellular protein, FKBP-12, which inhibits the protein kinase mTOR. This intracellular complex increases the expression of p27 and blocks the progression of the cell cycle from the G1 phase to the S phase (DNA synthesis). Taxanes are another class of drugs that interfere with microtubule function, which is necessary for the M phase (mitosis). As a result, cells are arrested in the G2 phase of the cell cycle.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cellular Receptors -- Cellular Level -- Intracellular Receptors
retrieved_docs.content: Type I intracellular receptors: In the inactive state, type I nuclear receptors are found in the cytoplasm and are usually bound to a chaperone protein. Following the binding of a ligand, the receptor undergoes a conformational change that releases the chaperone protein and allows the receptor to translocate to the nucleus. Once in the nucleus, the ligand-receptor complex binds to a specific DNA sequence and acts as a transcription factor to modulate the transcription of one or more genes. Type I intracellular receptors include the glucocorticoid, androgen, and progesterone receptors. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Ryanodine Receptor -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: A ryanodine receptor comprises 4 polypeptides, each consisting of around 5000 amino acids, and four FK506-binding proteins, each consisting of approximately 110 amino acids. [4] Moreover, the type 1 ryanodine receptor is the major isoform expressed in skeletal muscle, and the type 2 ryanodine receptor is the major isoform expressed in cardiac muscle. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Pharmacogenomics Overview -- Clinical Significance -- Post-Transcription Modification and Drug Candidates
retrieved_docs.content: Carriers of the KRAS mutation in non–small cell lung cancer lack a well-defined pocket on the KRAS protein surface where a small molecule can fit, making it challenging to design drugs. This characteristic is often referred to as a featureless protein surface, which has historically made KRAS considered an undruggable target. [63] The addition of cyclophilin A as a chaperone alters the conformation of the KRAS protein, making the target more approachable to drugs. This mechanism serves as an excellent example of post-transcriptional modifications in precision oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: Suicidal Ideation -- Etiology -- Genetic factors
retrieved_docs.content: Research in this area has focused on the FKBP5 gene variants implicated in depression and suicidal ideation. The results of systematic reviews and meta-analyses have found associations between these gene variants and increased risk of depressive disorders and suicidality, suggesting a genetic predisposition. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcineurin Inhibitors -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: CNIs generally bind with high affinity to specific cytoplasmic receptors known as immunophilins, which include cyclophilin and FK-binding proteins. [11] The drug-receptor complex effectively and competitively inhibits the activity of calcineurin, which is triggered by the intracellular calcium discharge following T-cell receptor interaction and co-stimulation facilitated by antigen-presenting cells. Subsequently, this process obstructs the translocation of a cluster of transcription factors, including the nuclear factor of activated T cells (NFAT), leading to reduced transcriptional initiation of cytokine genes such as interleukin (IL)-2, IL-3, IL-4, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, CD40 ligand (CD40L), granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor, and interferon-gamma. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcineurin Inhibitors -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Calcineurin inhibitors (CNIs) are a class of immunosuppressants utilized to effectively manage various autoimmune disorders, including, but not limited to, lupus nephritis, idiopathic inflammatory myositis, interstitial lung disease, and atopic dermatitis. In addition, they serve as essential components for immunosuppression in solid organ transplantation. CNIs generally bind with high affinity to specific cytoplasmic receptors known as immunophilins, which include cyclophilin and FK-binding proteins. Through targeted inhibition of calcineurin, these medications disrupt the transcription of interleukin-2 and other cytokines within T lymphocytes, thus disrupting the activation, proliferation, and differentiation of T cells. Although their primary effect is on T-helper cells, these inhibitors also concurrently inhibit T suppressor and T cytotoxic cells. This activity illustrates the indications, mechanism of action, and contraindications of CNIs, emphasizing their value in various conditions. This activity also highlights the essential elements such as off-label uses, dosing, pharmacokinetics, monitoring, and relevant interactions of CNIs that are relevant to healthcare professionals across various specialties in their daily clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Stents(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: DES consists of three components: a metallic stent platform, an active pharmacological drug agent, and a carrier vehicle. Stainless steel or cobalt-chromium is the most common metal and gives long-term mechanical stability to counteract vascular recoil. Commonly used drugs act to block signal transduction and cell cycle progression in different phases, thereby blocking smooth muscle cell (SMC) proliferation or intimal hyperplasia in the stented arterial site. Rapamycin agents bind to the intracellular protein, FKBP-12, that inhibits the protein kinase mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR). This intracellular complex increases the expression of p27 and blocks the progression of the cell cycle from the G1 phase to the S phase (DNA synthesis). Another drug category is taxanes, which interfere with microtubule function, which is necessary for the M phase (mitosis). So cells get arrested in the G2 phase of the cell cycle. To increase surface area, a carrier vehicle matrix or a polymer coating is used to enable sufficient drug loading and release for a long time. Polymer coating consists of repeating units of biodegradable poly-L-lactide, poly–D, and L-lactic-co-glycolic acid in a regular pattern, which are degraded into lactic acid and glycolic acid that ultimately get converted into water and carbon dioxide. First-generation DES has sirolimus or paclitaxel coating on a stainless steel base. In contrast, second-generation DES has zotarolimus or everolimus coating on top of biocompatible cobalt-chromium or platinum-chromium platform. The drug release is carried out by diffusion through pores in the polymeric coating.

retrieved_docs.title: Pimecrolimus -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: TCIs, by definition, inhibit calcineurin. Pimecrolimus achieves this as it binds to the FK506-binding protein (FKBP) and blocks calcineurin’s ability to dephosphorylate NFAT, effectively halting calcineurin-dependent transcription of genes for T-cell activation and production of cytokines such as interleukins 2, 4, and 10 as well as interferon-gamma.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcineurin Inhibitors -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The following points elucidate the mechanisms of action and distinct characteristics of various CNIs, such as cyclosporine, tacrolimus, pimecrolimus, and voclosporin, offering valuable insights into their roles in managing diverse conditions: Cyclosporine binds to cyclophilin-1 inside the cells to form the cyclosporine-cyclophilin complex. Subsequently, this complex inhibits calcineurin, which stops the activation and dephosphorylation of NFAT, a factor that plays a crucial role in triggering inflammatory reactions. [14] Tacrolimus and pimecrolimus share a common mechanism of action. Both compounds attach to FK-binding protein, thereby triggering the inhibition of calcineurin.

retrieved_docs.title: Suicidal Ideation -- Etiology -- Hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis dysfunction
retrieved_docs.content: Dysregulation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis, integral to stress response, is increasingly linked to mental health issues, particularly suicidal ideation. [21] This axis, involving a hormonal sequence from the hypothalamus, pituitary, and adrenal glands, controls cortisol release. Disruptions in this system, possibly due to epigenetic factors like early life trauma and gene variations (eg, FKBP5 gene affecting glucocorticoid receptor sensitivity), can lead to an imbalance in cortisol release. [22] Research on HPA axis dysregulation and suicidal ideation shows diverse results, indicating different cortisol responses in suicidal ideation subgroups. For instance, acute suicidal ideation cases may have higher cortisol increases than chronic ones, and a family history of suicide or suicide attempts is linked to reduced cortisol levels under stress, underscoring the HPA axis's complex role in suicidal ideation. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Methods of Estimation of Time Since Death -- Function -- Molecular Assessment
retrieved_docs.content: A study from Porto, Portugal, examined 11 gene transcripts for correlation with TSD. 8 murine tissues were divided into three groups based on the stability of the RNA - the first group (I) comprising of tissue samples from the heart, spleen, and lung, the second group (II) consisted of femoral quadriceps, liver and stomach and the third group (III) Pancreas and skin. Samples from groups I and II were serially analyzed. The analysis showed RNA degeneration was time-dependent for the entire 11 hours, although no statistical significance was demonstrable for the first four hours. Researchers selected 11 genes for quantitative PCR analysis. While RNA in the heart was found to be the most stable, it showed no correlation with PMI. Six genes were found to correlate with PMI, four in the femoral quadriceps ( Actb, Gapdh, Ppia, and Srp72 ), and two genes in the liver ( Alb and Cyp2E1 ). Mathematical models were developed to estimate PMI with an error mean of 51.4 minutes. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Pseudogenes -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Another mechanism of pseudogene function lies in its ability to affect mRNA stability through interactions with miRNA.  miRNA typically pairs with the 3’ untranslated region of an mRNA transcript and causes degradation or lower expression levels. One example of how pseudogene mRNA transcripts interact with miRNA is in the relationship between PTEN, a tumor suppressor, and its pseudogene PTENP1. In one study, the PTENP1 pseudogene mRNA binds to miRNA and has decreased the concentration of the functional miRNA within the cell. In this way, PTENP1 allows the PTEN mRNA to escape miRNA repression.  Another study is exploring the regulatory relationship between heat shock proteins, Hsp90, and its associated pseudogenes HSP90AA1 and HSP90AA2. Heat shock proteins actively express and account for two percent of all expressed proteins. These proteins, through microarray data, have numerous retrotransposed pseudogenes. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Charcot-Marie-Tooth Disease -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cytoskeletal structure: These genes are involved in actin polymerization ( INF2 ), stabilization of the myelin sheath through membrane-protein interactions ( PRX ), intermediate filaments ( NEFL ), cell signaling ( FGD4 ), and axonal transport ( DYNC1H1 ). Myelin-specific transcription factor: EGR2 is part of gene expression of myelin component proteins and Schwann cell differentiation. Nuclear-related genes: These genes include those encoding nuclear envelope proteins ( LMNA ), nucleotide biosynthesis enzymes, DNA repair factors, and proteins involved in cell survival. Endosomal sorting and cell signaling: These genes regulate vesicular transport, membrane trafficking, transport of intracellular organelles, and cell signaling. They include LITAF , MTMR2 , SBF1 , SBF2 , SH3TC2 , NDRG1 , FIG4 , RAB7 , TFG , DNM2 , and SIMPLE . Proteasome and protein aggregation: These genes regulate microtubules ( HSPB1 and HSPB8 ), cell adhesion ( LRSAM1 ), and ubiquitin ligase ( TRIM2 ).

Together, these relationships integrate pharmacologic classifications, immunologic hierarchies, and biochemical qualifiers, providing an integrated view of how antiarrhythmic, immunosuppressive, and biosynthesis‑related compounds interact and inform therapeutic and mechanistic understanding in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2021
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens are classified as progestogen sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, and they are considered members of the progesterone family. As such, they function as hormonal agents that influence reproductive physiology and genital system regulation, sharing the core structural and functional characteristics of progesterone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2695
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens are classified as members of the progesterone family, indicating that they share structural and functional characteristics with the hormone progesterone.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 518
retrieved_docs.content: Mifepristone is classified as a member of the group of progesterone receptor modulators, sex hormones, and modulators of the genital system.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 319
retrieved_docs.content: Medroxyprogesteronacetát is classified both as “antikoncepční látky ženské” (female contraceptive agents) and as “hormonální antikoncepční látky” (hormonal contraceptive agents), indicating that it belongs to these categories of contraceptive drugs. In addition, the human progesterone receptor is identified as a molecular target of medroxyprogesteronacetát, reflecting the drug’s mechanism of action through binding to this receptor to exert its contraceptive and hormonal effects. This interconnected knowledge highlights medroxyprogesteronacetát’s role within female and hormonal contraception and its specific interaction with the progesterone receptor in therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2171
retrieved_docs.content: Unknown Cellular or Molecular Interaction [MoA] is characterized by having the mechanism of action of jodid draselný.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2212
retrieved_docs.content: Alkylating agents are classified under the category of pharmacological effects—molecular mechanisms, indicating that their therapeutic action is understood within this mechanistic framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 139
retrieved_docs.content: and other pharmaceuticals are organized by chemical class, therapeutic function, and formulation context, aiding clinicians in drug selection, understanding mechanisms of action (particularly COX‑2 inhibition), and managing patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2288
retrieved_docs.content: Cyclosporine is classified as an enzyme inhibitor, indicating that it exerts its pharmacologic effects by inhibiting specific enzymatic pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1182
retrieved_docs.content: Progesterone receptor modulators, identified as sex hormones and modulators of the genital system, constitute a pharmacological class that includes the drug mifepristone. This class is classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) category “other sex hormones and modulators of the genital system,” indicating its placement among agents that act on hormonal pathways of the reproductive system. The inclusion of mifepristone in this class underscores its mechanism as a progesterone receptor modulator and its relevance to therapeutic strategies targeting genital system modulation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 771
retrieved_docs.content: Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens constitute a chemical class of steroid compounds, and medroxyprogesterone is identified as a member of this class. Moreover, the class of Pregnen (4) derivatives, progestogens itself is classified within the broader progesterone family, indicating that these progestogenic derivatives are subcomponents of the overall progesterone group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1372
retrieved_docs.content: Akarbóza, cyclosporin, and eflornithin are all classified as enzyme inhibitors, meaning each of these agents suppresses the activity of specific enzymes. Their inhibitory action influences metabolic pathways and can be leveraged therapeutically to modulate biological processes, making them relevant in various clinical and pharmacological contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1418
retrieved_docs.content: PITUITARY AND HYPOTHALAMIC HORMONES AND ANALOGUES are classified as hypothalamic hormones, encompassing both the natural hormones secreted by the pituitary and hypothalamus and their synthetic analogues. This categorization places them within the broader group of hypothalamic hormones, highlighting their shared origin and functional relevance in endocrine regulation and therapeutic applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 708
retrieved_docs.content: Estradiol is recognized as a specific hormone, indicating its role as a biologically active steroid that participates in endocrine signaling. Endocannabinoids are linked to the hormone category, suggesting that they either belong to a subclass of hormones or possess hormone‑like actions within the body. This relationship underscores the interconnected nature of steroid hormones such as estradiol and the endocannabinoid system, both of which contribute to the regulation of physiological processes through hormonal mechanisms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2099
retrieved_docs.content: Mifepristone is classified within the group of progesterone receptor modulators, sex hormones, and modulators of the genital system, indicating that it acts on hormonal pathways related to reproductive function. In addition, mifepristone functions as a hormone antagonist, meaning it blocks the action of certain endogenous hormones, particularly progesterone, thereby influencing processes that depend on progesterone signaling. This dual characterization highlights mifepristone’s role both as a member of a specific pharmacologic class and as an antagonist of hormonal activity, which is central to its therapeutic applications in medical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 31
retrieved_docs.content: not intersect with the prostaglandin network. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how prostaglandins function as a unifying biochemical and pharmacological framework that connects hormone metabolism, obstetric‑gynaecologic therapies, ocular pressure‑lowering drugs, cardiac treatments and downstream drug families, thereby guiding clinicians in selecting agents with shared structural and functional properties.
